paper_title: Why "more is better" fails: Contrasting scaling laws of bio-inspired spine arrays on soft and hard substrates
source_file: 机理文献/核心文献/06_Why more is better fails Contrasting scaling laws of bio-inspired spine arrays on soft and hard s.pdf
sections_checked: 4.3 Mechanisms of array-quantity effects on soft substrates
4.4 Mechanisms of array-quantity effects on hard substrates

equations_checked.notes: Retained as E1. Kappa=F_f/W=tan(phi+atan(f_s)); phi is measured from the vertical. The self-locking condition is stated separately for a total angle above 90 degrees.
Retained in E2. The added micro-damage angle theta appears inside the tangent. The formula is singular at 90 degrees and should not be numerically continued into the self-locking domain.
Retained in E2. Theta=arccos((R-h)/R) was visually verified; Fig. 8B gives h/R=0.15 as about 30 degrees and the text maps this to approximately 3.8 micrometers for R=25 micrometers.
Retained in grouped model E3. h_i=f(x_i,y_i), Z_s(0)=h_3, and Z_s=h_3-Delta Z(t) were visually checked. The paper does not define how h_3 is selected or ordered.
Retained in E3. Delta_i=max(0,h_i-Z_s(t)); N_contact is the indicator sum over 9 or 25 spines.
Retained in E3. W=sum(K_s delta_i) and W_average=W/N_contact were visually checked. K_s and its units are not reported, and plots label W in grams rather than force units.

figures_checked.reason: Biological and robot examples are background and do not establish a reusable mechanism.
Rig, material and qualitative soft/hard contact modes are adequately retained in text and parameter rows.
The distinct soft/hard peak-processing rules and raw-force character are preserved textually; the curves are not needed for model transfer.
Sequence stabilization is summarized by the first-three-strokes exclusion and the visible 20-sequence versus stated 50-set warning.
Combined with Fig. 6 to retain the irreducible soft inverted-U versus hard less-is-more experimental contrast; axes, legends, subpanels and captions were visually verified.
Combined with Fig. 5 as the minimum direct evidence for opposite substrate-dependent array scaling laws.
The soft-substrate state sequence and explicit 5 g/50 g onset times are compactly represented in mechanism M1 and validation V4.
Irreplaceable geometry linking damage depth to angle and showing spatial expansion of self-locking regions; crop includes all labels, color bars and caption and was visually verified.
Irreplaceable distinction between nominal count, real contact count, average load, and the evolving hard-substrate active set; crop includes all labels and caption and was visually verified.

omitted_sections: General biological examples and robot survey in the Introduction
Detailed instrument vendor information not affecting interpretation

uncertain_items.detail: The paper prints 'diameter of 0.63 x 5 mm', which is not a valid single diameter specification. The evidence card reproduces 0.63 x 5 mm without guessing which dimension is diameter or exposed length.

uncertain_items.location: PDF p.4 and p.8-9, Sections 2 and 4.2
uncertain_items.detail: Young's moduli, substrate strength or damage threshold, and the static friction coefficient f_s used in Fig. 8 are not reported. The soft-hard transition cannot be parameterized from this paper.
No direct damage imaging, depth measurement, or load-to-depth evolution is supplied. Theta and h are explanatory variables, not experimentally identified state variables.
The initial spine-plane height is set to h_3, but the text does not define the ordering or geometric selection of h_3. It must not be silently interpreted as the third-highest point.
K_s is not numerically defined and no units are given; W is a force symbol in the equations but the associated plots label W in grams. The model is structural, not quantitatively reproducible.

The method states 50 test sets per condition, but Fig. 4 displays sequence numbers only through 20 and the paper does not explain the subset. The first three strokes are discarded, but uncertainty bars or raw data are unavailable.

uncertain_items.location: PDF p.4 and p.6-8, Section 3, Fig. 5B and Section 4.1
uncertain_items.detail: Fig. 5B labels k3=0.198 and k5=0.452, and Section 4.1 calls S-3 the slowest condition. The Section 3 prose instead assigns 0.452 to S-3 and 0.198 to S-5. No slope value is used as a transferable parameter in the evidence card.

known_warnings: Array quantity, footprint, and average load per spine are confounded because total load is fixed and spacing stays at 5 mm.
Only one hard epoxy and one soft silicone are tested; there is no stiffness sweep or transition criterion.
The hard-substrate micro-damage model is a plausible explanation rather than a directly observed and calibrated damage law.
The tangent engagement equations require a separate self-locking state at the 90-degree singularity.
The soft contact-number model assumes a rigid horizontal plane, identical linear point stiffness, and excludes penetration despite being used to explain penetration experiments.
Soft and hard representative forces use different peak statistics, so absolute cross-material force values are not directly comparable.
The rigid common-base array does not represent independently compliant microspines, spring travel saturation, or post-failure load redistribution.
The synthetic fractal surface is under-specified by H=0.7 alone and is not a red-brick topography.
Fig. 5B/Section 4.1 and the Section 3 prose conflict on whether the S-3 or S-5 rapid-growth slope equals 0.198 versus 0.452 N/g.
character_count_method: Count of CJK Unified Ideographs after excluding display-formula blocks, Markdown table rows and image-link rows; updated during final validation.
